facts.label: FROM GREEN TO BLUE
facts.story: For over a decade the Blue Sharks weren't blue at all — Cape Verde wore green shirts until 1992, when the archipelago adopted a new national flag and swapped colors to match it. The rebrand gave the team its lasting identity: blue for the Atlantic surrounding ten volcanic islands, white islands scattered across the chest, red trim at the collar. The nickname Tubarões Azuis, the Blue Sharks, followed the kit change and never left.

facts.label: QUARTERFINALS ON DEBUT
facts.story: Cape Verde's football boom traces to a deliberate strategy: recruiting players from the Cape Verdean diaspora in Portugal, France, and the Netherlands to strengthen a nation of roughly half a million people. It paid off in 2013, when the Blue Sharks made their Africa Cup of Nations debut and stunned the continent by reaching the quarterfinals, falling only to Ghana in Port Elizabeth. That diaspora pipeline became the blueprint for every squad that followed.

2026.headline: Smallest Nation, Biggest Scare
2026.homeColors: deep blue shirt with red trim and triangular flight-path pattern, blue shorts, blue socks
2026.awayColors: white shirt with tonal grey flight-path pattern and blue/red detailing, white shorts, white socks

annotations.label: THE KEY MOMENT
annotations.story: Trailing to a 92nd-minute Lisandro Martínez header against reigning champions Argentina, Cape Verde looked finished — until Sidny Lopes Cabral drilled a stunning equalizer in the 103rd minute of extra time. Lionel Messi and a late own goal ultimately settled it 3-2, but Cape Verde came within minutes of the biggest upset in World Cup history.

annotations.label: THE KIT
annotations.story: Made by Capelli Sport rather than a major label, Cape Verde's kit builds its identity around 'Connected Through Rhythm' — a geometric triangle pattern tracing the flight paths linking the archipelago's ten islands. The deep blue home shirt carries red trim; the white away kit mirrors it in tonal grey with blue-and-red accents.

annotations.label: THE CONTEXT
annotations.story: Cape Verde became the smallest nation — by population, roughly half a million people — to reach a World Cup knockout round, and the first debutant to advance from the group stage since Slovakia in 2010. The run leaned on a diaspora pipeline of players raised in Portugal, France and the Netherlands.
